content.problemStatement:
Everybody loves magic, especially magicians who compete for glory on the Byteland Magic Tournament. Magician Cyael is one such magician.

Cyael has been having some issues with her last performances and today she‚Äôll have to perform for an audience of some judges, who will change her tournament ranking, possibly increasing it. As she is a great magician she managed to gather a description of the fixed judges‚Äô disposition on the room (which is represented as an N√óNN \times NN√óN square matrix), such that she knows in advance the fixed points each judge will provide.

She also knows that the room is divided into several parallel corridors, such that we will denote the jthj^{th}jth cell on corridor iii, as [i][j][i][j][i][j]. Note that some judges can award Cyael, zero points or negative points, as they are never pleased with her performance. There is just one judge at each cell of the matrix, except the cells [1][1][1][1][1][1] and [N][N][N][N][N][N].
To complete her evaluation, she must start on the top leftmost corner of the room (cell [1][1][1][1][1][1]), and finish on the bottom right corner (cell [N][N][N][N][N][N]), moving either to the cell directly in front of her on the same corridor (that is, moving from cell [r][c][r][c][r][c] to cell [r][c+1][r][c+1][r][c+1], where c+1c+1c+1 ‚â§\leq‚â§ NNN) or to the cell in the next corridor directly in front of where she is (that is, moving from cell [r][c][r][c][r][c] to cell [r+1][c][r+1][c][r+1][c], where r+1r+1r+1 ‚â§\leq‚â§ NNN). She will keep doing this until she reaches the end point of the room, i.e. last cell [N][N][N][N][N][N] on the last corridor. Cyael will be judged at all visited cells with a judge.

Cyael wants to maximize her average score at end of her performance. More specifically, if she passes KKK judges, each being on cell [i1][j1][i_{1}][j_{1}][i1‚Äã][j1‚Äã], cell [i2][j2][i_{2}][j_{2}][i2‚Äã][j2‚Äã], ‚Ä¶\ldots‚Ä¶, cell [iK][jK][i_{K}][j_{K}][iK‚Äã][jK‚Äã] respectively, then she wants to maximize (S[i1][j1]+S[i2][j2]+‚Ä¶+S[iK][jK])K\frac{(S[i_{1}][j_{1}] + S[i_{2}][j_{2}] + \ldots + S[i_{K}][j_{K}])}{K}K(S[i1‚Äã][j1‚Äã]+S[i2‚Äã][j2‚Äã]+‚Ä¶+S[iK‚Äã][jK‚Äã])‚Äã, where S[i][j]S[i][j]S[i][j] denotes the points that the judge will give her on the cell [i][j][i][j][i][j].
Help her determine the best path she has to follow in order to maximize her average points.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description for TTT test cases follows.
For each test case, the first line contains a single integer NNN.
Each of the next NNN lines contains NNN space-separated integers. The j-th integer S[i][j]S[i][j]S[i][j] in i-th line denotes the points awarded by the judge at cell [i][j][i][j][i][j].
Note that the cells [1][1][1][1][1][1] and [N][N][N][N][N][N] have no judges, so S[1][1]S[1][1]S[1][1] and S[N][N]S[N][N]S[N][N] will be 0.


Output Format

For each test case, if the maximum possible average points Cyael can obtain is negative, output a single line containing "Bad Judges" (quotes for clarity).
Otherwise, output the maximum possible average points.
The answer will be considered correct if it has an absolute error no more than  10‚àí610^{-6}10‚àí6.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains a single integer N. Each of the next N lines contains N space-separated integers. The j-th integer S[i][j] in the i-th line denotes the points awarded by the judge at cell [i][j].
content.outputFormat: For each test case, if the maximum possible average points Cyael can obtain is negative, output a single line containing 'Bad Judges'. Otherwise, output the maximum possible average points.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 1: An optimal path for Cyael would be (1,1)‚Üí(2,1)‚Üí(2,2). This way Cyael faces 1 judge and gets a total score of 8. Thus, the average score is 8/1 = 8.
Test case 2: No matter what path Cyael chooses, the final score would be less than 0.

Explanation:
Test case 1: An optimal path for Cyael would be (1,1)‚Üí(2,1)‚Üí(3,1)‚Üí(3,2)‚Üí(3,3). This way Cyael faces 4 judges and gets a total score of 18. Thus, the average score is 18/4 = 4.5.

Explanation:
Test case 1: An optimal path for Cyael would be (1,1)‚Üí(2,1)‚Üí(3,1)‚Üí(4,1)‚Üí(4,2)‚Üí(4,3)‚Üí(4,4). This way Cyael faces 6 judges and gets a total score of 48. Thus, the average score is 48/6 = 8.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we use dynamic programming to find the maximum possible average points Cyael can obtain. We create a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] represents the maximum points Cyael can get from the top-left corner to the cell [i][j]. We fill up the dp array by iterating over each cell and considering the maximum points we can get from the cell above it and the cell to its left. Finally, we calculate the average points by dividing the maximum points by the total number of cells visited.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * N^2) per test case, O(T * N^2) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) ‚Äî the dominant structure is the dp array.
